title: Per-tile grid-origin and expectedLen recomputed twice in the same function for cache hits and partial-hit paths
description: In decodeTiledViewportPooled, the grid tile origin (col, row, gridTileX, gridTileY, gridTileW, gridTileH) and expectedLen are computed once in the cache-scan loop (lines ~1174-1181) and again identically when stitching hits in the all-hits fast path (lines ~1199-1207) and again for the partial-hit stitch (lines ~1280-1287). For viewports covering many tiles this is a low-cost but avoidable triple computation. Storing these as a precomputed list alongside 'hits' would eliminate the duplicate arithmetic.

title: Per-tile grid-origin arithmetic duplicated in dc-phase and final-phase of progressive dc-then-final direct path
description: In the progressive dc-then-final path of decodeTiledViewport, each of the two runWithBoundedConcurrency loops independently recomputes tx, ty, srcOriginX, srcOriginY, decodedW, decodedH, and expectedLen for every tile (lines ~250-258 in phase 1, lines ~341-348 in phase 2). These values are purely a function of the tile rect and source geometry, and they are identical across both phases for each tile. Pre-extracting them once alongside tileBytesList (which is already hoisted at line 209) would halve these multiplications for every tile in every dc-then-final viewport decode.

title: O(n) deduplication of failedTiles using Set<string> + tileKey() allocates per-tile strings at end of decode
description: After the progressive decode phases, a de-duplication pass iterates failedTiles and calls tileKey(id) on every entry to build a string-keyed Set. For the common case (zero or one failure) this is harmless, but for skip-tile paths on a large tiled viewport the set is built from string allocations after the hot decode loop. Since failedTiles is already accumulated from phase-1 and phase-2 separately for the same tile, using a Set<number> keyed by tileKeyPacked() throughout the accumulation phase (which returns a pre-cached integer) would eliminate these end-of-loop string allocations.

title: Two intermediate arrays (tileBytesList + tilesWithBytes) allocated for every progressive viewport decode
description: The progressive dc-then-final path allocates tileBytesList via plan.tiles.map() and then immediately allocates tilesWithBytes via a second plan.tiles.map() that pairs each tile with its bytes and index. These two allocations could be merged into a single map pass. For typical 3x3=9 tile viewports this is minor, but for large tiled viewports (e.g. 8000px image with 512px tiles = up to 16×16=256 tiles) the double allocation is measurable.

title: Center-out sort allocates a new array and object per tile on every viewport decode
description: tilesWithBytes.slice().sort() allocates a new array of the same size, and the comparator computes (x + w/2 - cx)**2 + (y + h/2 - cy)**2 inline for each comparison without caching. JavaScript sort is O(n log n) with O(n) extra comparator invocations, each performing 4 multiplications and 2 additions. For a 16×16 tile grid this is 256 tiles × ~log2(256)×2 = ~2000 comparator calls. Pre-computing the squared distance for each tile once (O(n)) before sorting would reduce comparator cost to a single subtraction.

title: Center-out sort of misses array allocates and recomputes per viewport decode in pooled path
description: decodeTiledViewportPooled sorts the misses array with misses.slice().sort() and an inline squared-distance comparator (same pattern as the direct path). This allocation and O(n log n) sort with repeated arithmetic runs on every viewport pan event. The same pre-computation optimization applies: compute the center distance for each tile once and store it before the sort.

title: _reapBound uses Array.includes() (O(n)) to check idle membership on every timer fire
description: The idle-reap timer callback calls this.idle.includes(h), which is O(n) on the idle array. For small pools (maxSize=8) this is negligible, but the idle array check happens on every idle timeout expiry. A Set<WorkerHandle> for the idle list, or storing a boolean 'inIdle' flag on the handle, would make this O(1). The current LIFO array was chosen for cache hotness (Grok3 #18 comment), but the includes() scan can be avoided while keeping LIFO.

title: destroyHandle uses Array.indexOf() + Array.splice() to remove handle from idle list (O(n) scan)
description: destroyHandle scans the idle array with this.idle.indexOf(h) on every handle teardown. On a visibility-hidden event that calls reapAllIdle(), this degenerates to O(n^2) over the idle pool. For small pools (maxSize=8) this is trivial, but it could be made O(1) with a doubly-linked list or a generation-stamped approach while preserving LIFO semantics.

title: setHandleState allocates a new allowed[] array on every state transition via object literal
description: setHandleState is called frequently during decode (once per tile acquire + once per release) and constructs a fresh Record<HandleState, HandleState[]> object literal every call, including 5 array literals. In production (DEV=false) the guard short-circuits and only the assignment runs, but the object construction still occurs on every call regardless of whether DEV is true. Hoisting the allowed table as a module-level const would eliminate these per-call allocations.

title: destroy() iterates pending jobs via Array.from(h.pending.values()) allocating a snapshot array on teardown
description: Both the destroy() path and the on-failure path in decodeTilesParallel call Array.from(hh.pending.entries()) or Array.from(h.pending.values()) to snapshot the pending map before iterating. This is correct (prevents mutation-during-iteration), but the Array.from() allocates a new array whose size is proportional to pending jobs. For the normal destroy path this is a one-time cost, but the failure-path snapshot in decodeTilesParallel (line ~1013) runs for every tile batch failure and allocates one array per handle. Iterating with .forEach() or for..of on a copy is equivalent and avoids this.

title: cacheStore always calls pixels.slice() even when byteLength == need (no surplus)
description: cacheStore calls pixels.slice() unconditionally when byteLength > need (to trim), and pixels.slice() again when byteLength == need (a full copy even though trimming is not needed). The comment implies slicing is always desired for ownership safety, but for the common case where the caller already owns the pixels (e.g. the progressive path writes into a dedicated target array), this copy is wasted. A zero-copy store path could be gated on an opt-in flag, or the trim branch could at least short-circuit when byteLength == need to a plain slice() without the length check overhead.

title: Per-tile final-cache and dc-cache lookups in Phase 1 of progressive path cause N independent cache.get() calls with N string key allocations
description: For each tile in Phase 1, the code constructs a finalKey string via template literal (`${makeTileCacheKey(levelId, id)}:${plan.format}:final`) and a separate dcKey string, then calls cache.get() twice per tile. With N tiles in a viewport, this is 2N string allocations and 2N map lookups on the hot pan path. The per-tile finalKey construction is also repeated identically in Phase 2 (line ~351), yielding 3N total key constructions per progressive decode. Pre-building all tile keys into a Map<string, CacheHitInfo> in a single pass before either phase would reduce this to N key constructions and cache lookups.

title: bytesId constructs a template-literal string on every call including the hot cache lookup path via getLevelId
description: bytesId() is called via getLevelId() on every cache key derivation, and it constructs a `:${b}:${view.byteOffset}:${view.byteLength}` string. While this is a single template-literal (not a regex), for the tiled-viewport cache path it is called for every viewport decode even when the ID is already cached in the WeakMap. The WeakMap hit path (when bufIdByBuffer already has an entry) still allocates the composite string each time. Caching the full composite string in a second WeakMap<Uint8Array, string> keyed by view identity (not just buffer) would make repeat calls O(1) with zero string allocation.

title: No tile-decode throughput benchmark or regression gate exists for the jxl-pyramid package
description: The pan-60fps test in decode-level.test.ts exercises decode correctness and has a very loose wall-clock guard (< 30s for 24 frames). There is no benchmark or CI gate that measures tiles/second or ms-per-viewport for typical pyramid sizes (e.g. 8000px image, 512px tiles, 3x3 viewport). Without a regression gate, the per-tile alloc and sort regressions (perf-4 through perf-6) can silently accumulate. Adding a bun bench or similar harness that asserts decode throughput stays above a baseline would catch regressions during code review.

title: decodeTilesParallel coro fan-out creates one async closure per handle, each capturing the tile queue via shared mutable 'next' counter
description: decodeTilesParallel creates handles.length async closures via handles.map(async (h) => { while (true) {...} }). Each closure is a heap-allocated async function instance with a captured environment. For a typical 2-4 handle pool this is 2-4 closures per viewport decode. This is fine for the pooled parallel path, but for the progressive dc-then-final path the fan-out runs twice (once for dc, once for final), allocating 4-8 closures total per viewport. This is a minor but visible allocation on the decode hot path when profiling.
